title: Debugging a Pending Pod
description:
## Debugging a Pending Pod

A pod stuck in `Pending` state cannot be scheduled. Common causes:
- **Insufficient resources** — no node has enough CPU/memory
- **No matching nodeSelector** — label doesn't exist on any node
- **Taint without toleration** — node repels the pod
- **Unbound PVC** — pod waits for storage

The `Events` section of `kubectl describe pod` is the fastest way to identify the cause.

**Your task:**

A Pod named `stuck-pod` is stuck in `Pending`. Investigate why and fix it.

```bash
# Start your investigation:
kubectl get pod stuck-pod
kubectl describe pod stuck-pod
```

> 💡 Look at the `Events` section at the bottom of the `describe` output.

hints.title: Read the Events section
hints.body: kubectl describe pod shows a Warning event from the scheduler explaining why it cannot place the pod. The message will mention the specific constraint that cannot be satisfied.
hints.command: kubectl describe pod stuck-pod | grep -A 10 Events

hints.title: The pod has a nodeSelector that doesn't match any node
hints.body: Check what nodeSelector the pod is requesting, then check the labels on the available nodes.
hints.command: kubectl get pod stuck-pod -o jsonpath='{.spec.nodeSelector}' && kubectl get nodes --show-labels

hints.title: Fix: remove the bad nodeSelector
hints.body: Delete and recreate the pod without the nodeSelector.
hints.command: kubectl delete pod stuck-pod --ignore-not-found --force && kubectl run stuck-pod --image=nginx:alpine

setup_commands.command:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: stuck-pod
spec:
  nodeSelector:
    disk: nvme-does-not-exist
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:alpine
EOF

commands.command: kubectl get pod stuck-pod -o jsonpath='{.spec.nodeSelector.disk}' 2>/dev/null || echo ''
